speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, Henry--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is an answer nobody can give. It is sheer luck or a miracle, or whatever you want to call it. One who survived couldn't say he was smart. He did this. He did that. That wasn't the case. Sheer luck. Whatever you want to call it, you believe. You say it's God's will. And you can take it either way. I have no answer for that except to say I was a lucky person. A bullet didn't hit me. It just missed, and I ducked, and that's it. But other than that, I don't know what to answer, how to say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Shoah often or sometimes? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me say this. 50 years elapsed, over 50 years since the liberation, 51 years. I still have nightmares at times, and my wife has to wake me up and say, it's OK. It's OK. It's OK. So-- so even without thinking about it, whenever we gather, we get together, Holocaust survivors, the talk is about the Holocaust, what happened during the war in the camps. No matter what we start talking about, about bar mitzvahs, about wedding, the bottom line is everybody comes back to the Holocaust story. So this will never ever-- we'll live with that, and we will live till the end of our lives with that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is simple. First of all, our daughter, the younger daughter, Sandy, she wants to know exactly what happened. In fact, she gave-- bought her mother, for one birthday, a notebook, and gave her a pen, and says, Mom, write down from what happened in the country. She tried to ask me. I said, Sandy, forget it. I don't want to do it. Whatever. And when this came about, when they announced in our Holocaust organization with these sheets. You have an 800 number if you want to go and what it's all about, that it's going to be preserved for the future. My wife and I, we figured, let's tell our story. Let the deniers be confronted with videotapes or whatever Mr. Spielberg is going to produce. That-- that made us convinced to leave a legacy somehow, whether it's videotape or whatever. And that why we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, is there a message you would like to give to your children or grandchildren about your experiences, about how those experiences affected you, or to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I would like to give a message. I prepared something. No, might not have it now. Don't tell me I lost it. I must have dropped it in the restaurant. Oh, maybe not. No, I'm sorry. It's OK, I have it right here. I prepared it. I want to prepare something. It's not only a message to my children, it's a message to all mankind. The few words that I would like to say, let me think. Let me read it to you. 'I would like to take this opportunity to express my gratitude and to let you know what has brought me great satisfaction since the end of World War II. I married my wife, Rose, 50 years ago, in 1946. And together, our journey has led us to many places and times of joy. In 1948, our daughter, Chana, was born. And the three of us, in 1949, crossed the Atlantic and arrived in the greatest country in the world, the United States of America. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wish to thank the government of the United States, not only for their efforts in World War II, but also for allowing my family the chance to live a fulfilling life. After our experiences with tyranny and oppression, the words 'life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness' have great meaning for me. In America, we have been free to practice our religion, to vote, to work hard, to pursue security for our children without fear. Thank you, Uncle Sam. Our second daughter, Sandy, was born in 1952 in the Lower East Side of New York City. She, like Chana, was able to attend great public schools and universities and has never had to experience the horrors of our younger years. Chana, Sandy, and my three grandsons, Michael, Benjamin, and Jesse, bring Rose and me great joy, and I wish to thank them. I would like to thank Mr. Steven Spielberg. His inspiration has brought great meaning to all of us who have survived. Because of his efforts, no one will forget and no one can deny. I would like to thank the filming crew, Miss [? Oslo, ?] and Mr. [? Omen, ?] the people who have come to carry out this project. My wife, my children, and my grandchildren-- thank you all from the bottom of our hearts.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much. That's the end. Thank you, Henry. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And you're going to introduce us to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my dear wife, Rose Goteiner, whom I married 50 years ago. It's a pleasure to introduce you to you, to tell you her story. Whatever question you'll ask of her, I'm sure she will.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rose, you've been married 50 years. What has it been like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It was a joy, it was pain, but we went through it together. But thank God, at the end, everything worked out well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war in Zeilsheim. I was young. He was young. And after the war, everybody was looking for somebody to be attached to. Simple as that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in Germany. We had a child in Germany because took so long to come to the United States, that we think we never will get here. And after she was six months, we got the notification that we should get ready to leave for United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are also a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll be talking to you on another tape shortly. How has your experience in the Shoah, yours and Henry's, affected your lives now? And you can both answer if you like. How did they affect your lives over the years?

speaker: interviewee
text: How it did affect it? It is painful to think about it. We're trying to forget. Doesn't work. I think as long as we will live, we will die with it. We try not to talk about it, but somehow it comes out, and we always do talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe on one way it's better. Because being together with the survivors and talking about it-- maybe we don't need psychiatrists. Who knows? Because you're reliving-- reliving the emotions what you had when the war came and everything. So maybe this-- that's good part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your family, your children and grandchildren, would you like to tell us something about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can say this. Our children are a joy to us. Our grandchildren, in one plain Yiddish word-- we are having naches from them. Lots of naches. We're enjoying our family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After the liberation, I could tell you this. I would never, ever believe that I would be married 50 years and still be alive. I thought we were not going to last long. However, we have defeated Hitler, [SPEAKING HEBREW]

speaker: interviewer
text: Translation?

speaker: interviewee
text: May his name be erased from the face of the Earth. That's the translation from Hebrew to English. And with this, I say amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amen. And we'll now be meeting your children and grandchildren through pictures. And maybe you'll have some pictures of former times, as well. I know you don't have all of your pictures here, but we're going to take a look. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Henry, this is a picture of whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my father and my mother. His name was called Shlomo Fischel Gotajner, and my mother was Chana Dina Gotajner. The ages, I cannot tell you. However, my father was older than my mother because he had a previous marriage. He was previously married to another woman, who had also a few children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in the city of Lodz Poland. It could be in the early '30s or in the late '20s.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can start right in, Henry. And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife's father. His name was Yeheskel-- Yiddish, Chaskel-- Konigstein. And he looks here, still a young man, a good looking man. And he was taken away by the Germans very early in his life. He was really short-changed. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it could have been taken in Lodz because I see-- at this age, I don't think he was in Upper Silesia yet. So I think that it was also taken in Lodz because her father, my wife's father, came from Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would you estimate?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would also consider it in the early '30s or in the late '20s. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife's mother. She was a young woman yet. I think it was maybe after the marriage. Her name is Shaindl Miriam Konigstein. And she got-- they got married. They moved to Upper Silesia. That's a part of Poland that was-- Oberschlesien, it was called. And that's where they started, actually, a family there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date? Oh, no, this could have been before-- let's-- this-- in the 1900s. I could not say the exact year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is my wife's aunts-- aunt and uncle and a son, their son, which is-- makes my wife's cousin. They were living at that time, I believe, also in Upper Silesia, not far where my wife's parents lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date, it must have been in the 1900s, in the beginning. 1915, 1918, something like that, I would estimate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my wife's parents and her younger siblings, including her in this picture. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you name them from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. From left to right? From left to right, is-- sitting is her brother, this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Herman?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, from left to right. No, this is Fredek. And Fredek is from left to right. Then it's Ruthka, her sister. And then standing from left to right is my wife, Rose, who's named [? Ruzha ?] Konigstein, her mother, Shaindl Konigstein, her father, Yeheskel Konigstein. And the extreme right is Herman, her brother. In fact, he's got there like a Band-Aid on his cheek, on the right side of the cheek. This is-- he was shot by the Germans. He wouldn't let them in, to let them come into their apartment-- that's a story in itself-- till the parents escaped through the window. So he was a brave-- a brave boy. Too bad he did not survive. Too bad anybody of them did-- did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- somewhere in Oberschlesien. I don't know exactly the town. Maybe it was Katowice or another area near that, but it was in that area there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date was in-- in the '40s, maybe '41, '42, or '43, either one of these dates. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a picture of--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of when, in 1946, October '46, when my wife and I got married. On the right, that's me. On the left is my wife, which I love dearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, in Frankfurt am Main [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date, October 29, 1946. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older daughter, Chana, the oldest. And this is her husband, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live now in Rockville, Maryland. And they're happily married, and they have two sons. The older son is Michael and the younger son is Benjamin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are--

speaker: interviewee
text: These are-- this is my youngest daughter, Sandy, and this is her husband next her, James. And this is her son, Jesse.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- oh, this, I really don't remember, but I see it's some kind of affair. I don't recall where it was taken. But the boy looks eight years old and he is now 12. So it was about four years ago taken. Exactly where, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live right here in Boca Raton, not far from us. About a 10, 12-minute drive, and we're happy to live close to them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken recently, a couple months back on October, when we had our 50th wedding anniversary. Our daughters insisted we should have a-- a wedding anniversary, and they rented a place in New York, in Queens, which is called Cafe Baba. And we arranged, and we had some friends there with us. And we enjoyed the evening very much. Very good, very nice with our children, some family, and mostly friends. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are--

speaker: interviewee
text: These are our three grandchildren, which we love very much. From the right, sitting, is Michael, my oldest grandson, Chana's son. Behind him, with the long hair, that's Benjamin, his brother, also my grandson. And then I have, from the other daughter, little Jesse. We also all love him very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken where? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not recall exactly. However, it looks Florida. It could have been here when they came in, probably for a Passover reunion. Mostly we have all the family here at Passover. We get it here in our place. So it could be somewhere in Florida here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is us, the grandparents, which we are very happy we are joined by our three grandsons. And as you can see in the faces, all smiling, all happy. And we wish them happiness and long life. They should live, should never know from the horrors that we went through, and I hope that will be the case.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of my oldest grandson, Michael. Now, of course, he is married to this young lady, Jessica. So this is a couple now. And it's a pleasure to see them together. They're living like love birds. They cherish each other very much. They went together of college, and they got to know each other. And they got so close enough that they even got married. So we are happy for them.
